name: Granule
summary: Research language whose IR layers graded modal types on top of linearity: a [n] means use a exactly n times where n ranges over a user-chosen semiring (naturals, intervals, security levels, capabilities). Co-effects beyond linearity, expressed as graded annotations on every binding.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Granule's IR layers *graded modal types* on top of linearity: `a [n]` means "use `a` exactly `n` times" where `n` ranges over a user-chosen semiring (naturals, intervals, security levels, capabilities). The IR carries these grades on every binding and propagates them through every operation.

sections.title: Grades vs linearity
sections.content:
The point of grades is to express co-effects — properties of how a value is consumed — that simple linearity cannot. Security levels (low, high) distinguish public and secret data; intervals bound how many times a function can be called.

Granule remains research-grade but its IR design is a clean exemplar of how graded modal types layer onto a typed lambda calculus. The user-chosen semiring is the key feature: rather than baking in one specific co-effect (linearity, security, count-of-uses), the IR lets each application choose the resource semiring relevant to its problem.
